CRF can provide performance improvements with only a modest overhead compared to the original DNN in a range of applications. The hybrid approach combining CRF and DNN resulted in a performance improvement over the pure DNN architecture.
CRFs represent a joint probability distribution over a structured output given data observations, factorizing as a product of nonnegative potential functions over a given graph structure.
The potential functions in CRFs are defined over a given graph structure, with nonnegative pairwise potentials encouraging local consistency and agreement between the variables.
LSM potential functions are described as log-supermodular functions that encourage agreement between the variables they operate on.
A function f is considered log-supermodular if f(x)f(y) ≤ f(x ∧ y)f(x ∨ y) for all x, y in D.
If the inequality from the definition of log-supermodular is reversed, the function f is said to be log-submodular.
A twice continuously differentiable function is log-supermodular if the off-diagonal elements of the Hessian matrix of log(f) are nonnegative over the domain.

Similarities between random variables in continuous models are often modeled using a multivariate Gaussian distribution.
Multivariate Gaussians representing a simple, well-understood parametric family.
Multivariate Gaussians are associated with Gaussian CRFs, which admit exact MAP inference via matrix inversion.
Gaussian CRFs admit exact MAP inference via matrix inversion.
In special sub-classes of Gaussian models, exact MAP inference can be done with Gaussian belief propagation in walk-summable Gaussian graphical models.
An approach based on walk-summable models involves potentials of the form exp(-(yi - vi)^2) where vi is the mean parameter of a Gaussian potential.
An approach based on walk-summable models involves parameterized pairwise potentials of the form exp(-(wij * (yi - yj)^2)).
The parameterized pairwise potentials are modeled with parameters given as output of the parameter NN.
The nonnegativity constraint on the parameters ensures that the joint distribution is a walk-summable Gaussian.
